data.title: Church & Dwight: Hammered Down As Expected, Upgrading To Hold
data.description:
Intro

In summer, on 22 July 2025, I threw a look at Church & Dwight (CHD) stock for the first time. Back then, I rated the stock a "sell," expecting shares to decline. This was a correct call, as CHD stock continued to decline from its highs made earlier this year, being down about 14% since my analysis.

After this decline, CHD stock is now down even over a five-year period, not counting dividends.

Investors and analysts might wonder, when this correction will be over. After having gone through the recent Q3 results, I upgrade my rating from "sell" to "hold". CHD is performing better than many peers who are coping with weak pricing and volume developments. Not so for Church & Dwight.

First signs of a stabilization

Church & Dwight reported robust Q3 2025 results, exceeding expectations amid a volatile consumer environment that's causing headaches for many consumer staples companies. But some, like CHD, are competing better. Both, the stock price (smaller declines) and especially the operating results, do reflect that. CHD is building positive momentum, as I will show now.

Revenue reached $1.59 billion, up 5% year-over-year, surpassing consensus estimates of $1.53 billion. Organic sales grew 3.4%, driven by 2.5% volume gains and 0.9% from pricing and mix, fueled by strong performances in core brands like Arm & Hammer laundry and the recently acquired Touchland hand sanitizer. The latter posted double-digit growth. This is important – Church & Dwight showed both, volume AND pricing strength.

As a result, CHD upped its previously too cautious guidance.

Adjusted EPS rose 2.5% to $0.81, supported by gross margin expansion. Gross margins are still expected to decline modestly, but less than previously guided, thanks to strong progress on efficiency and cost control. Productivity initiatives delivered 170 basis points of savings, offsetting 200 basis points of inflation and tariff pressures. CHD is not complaining, but acting.

Operating cash flow surged 19.6% to $435.5 million, with year-to-date share repurchases totaling $600 million, reflecting confidence in capital allocation, and making use of the lower share price. Just back the envelope, $600 million at the current market cap of $20 billion is 3% of shares outstanding. That's an acceptable pace, noticeably benefiting per-share results.

EPS over the last twelve months reached one their highest levels over the last five years. With strong operating momentum and buybacks in full swing, it should be only a matter of time until a new high in EPS can be reported. The guidance for the year sees $3.49.

Sooner or later, this will be reflected in the stock price.

Other achievements included market share gains in key categories (as said, many consumer staples companies are struggling) and successful integration of acquisitions. Increased marketing investments were absorbed without eroding profitability, while positive retailer line reviews signal sustained shelf strength, as was said during the conference call.

Cash flow is ticking up, having reached $1.14 billion over the last twelve months. Management, after the strong third quarter, upped the outlook for cash flow, now expected to be $1.2 billion for the full year.

Challenges remain, though. Notably, the planned discontinuation of low-margin businesses, expected to reduce Q4 sales by $30 million (200 bps impact) as inventory clears. This should be only short-term noise, strengthening CHD’s profile and margins over time. Macro volatility and input cost pressures persist, though largely mitigated through successful operational execution.

For the full year 2025, CHD raised adjusted EPS guidance to $3.49 (up $0.02) and cash flow from operations to ~$1.2 billion, while holding organic sales growth at 1% due to, or, seen positively, despite the Q4 headwind. Q4 sales are projected to grow ~3.5%, supported by innovation and acquisitions.

Looking ahead, management expressed optimism around portfolio resilience and bolt-on M&A opportunities, with an Investor Day in January to outline long-term growth strategies. Overall, CHD demonstrated defensive strength and operational excellence in a challenging landscape.

That's why I reward this positive development with an upgrade from "sell" to "hold".

CHD stock, though not dirt-cheap, is now showing a much better setup. After the share price decline, the valuation has improved. With an enterprise value of ~22 billion ($20 billion market cap + ~$1.9 billion net debt), vs. operating cash flow of $1.2 billion (~$1.1 billion FCF), the FCF multiple has reached 20x, or an expected FCF yield of 5%. There is room for improvement – that's why I don't give a "buy" rating – but together with the convincing operating performance and solid single-digit growth (supported by buybacks), high single-digit returns are possible with CHD stock over time.

But for a better rating, I'd like to see the valuation drop more.

Risks to My Thesis

As CHD stock has shown a strong performance recently, beating estimates and its own previous guidance, it is possible defensively oriented investors discover this consumer staples jewel, sending the stock price higher (my rating would be too defensive in this case).

The opposite could happen, too. An unexpected consumer backdrop and/or a second wave of input cost pressure could compress margins again, and with it, the stock price.

Although not my highest-probability scenario, CHD could be acquired by a bigger company, as $20 billion in equity value is not a hurdle high enough. This would be great for shareholders, but invalidate my current rating.

Conclusion

Church & Dwight is showing a strong operational performance and positive momentum. I like the fact that CHD is able to push both, volumes and prices, proving its strength. In consequence, I am upgrading my previous "sell" rating to a solid "hold".

data.title: Domino's: Finally Time To Bite (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In today's stock market, timing and stock selection are key. In my view, you won't earn much in 2026 by simply holding the S&P 500, but there are pockets of opportunity in several unloved industries.

In the food and restaurant space, Domino's (DPZ) is quickly emerging as a winner in a bloodbath for restaurant stocks in the Q3 earnings season. Despite the fact that Domino's itself posted very strong Q3 results, backlash against the industry after a string of poor earnings from the likes of Chipotle (CMG) and CAVA (CAVA) has spilled over to Domino's as well, and now the stock is down for the year, creating a potential buying opportunity.

I last wrote a neutral article on Domino's in August, when the stock was trading higher at $450 per share, upgrading the stock from a prior sell rating. Today, with the share of Domino's even lower despite acceleration in systemwide sales growth (a notable achievement in a quarter where most restaurants have reported resoundingly weaker growth rates), I'm upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

For investors who are newer to Domino's, here are the core reasons to be long on this stock:

  • Winning U.S. market share in a difficult climate. Many U.S. restaurant chains are reporting vast slowdowns in comp sales growth, while Domino's is reporting the opposite: same-store sales acceleration. The company is achieving this success via a continued focus on delivery and off-premises revenue, as well as promotions that are appealing to value-conscious customers (and despite these promotions, average ticket prices still rose 1.3% in Q3).
  • Asset light. There are more than 21k Domino's locations across the world, and Domino's itself only owns 260 stores in the U.S., or just over 1% of its store footprint. The company's expansion, driven primarily by franchisees, is not limited by its own balance sheet.
  • Aggressive expansion plans in China and India. The company is targeting to open 300 stores a year in China and 200 a year in India. Domino’s franchise-first strategy positions the company for high-margin recurring revenue streams without direct risk.
  • Delivery network that is not reliant on partners. Where many restaurants are crumbling now is in their reliance on the likes of Uber Eats (UBER) and DoorDash (DASH), whose fees are eating into their margins. Domino's recently rolled out its DoorDash partnership, which it expects to be an incremental acceleration to U.S. comps in Q4, adding on top of an existing agreement with Uber Eats. That said, the fact that the company has its own delivery services gives it a keen competitive advantage.

Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q3 Recap

Let's now go through Domino's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Domino's revenue grew 6% y/y to $1.15 billion, which beat Wall Street's expectations of $1.14 billion (+5% y/y) by a one-point margin. Meanwhile, revenue growth also accelerated one point versus 5% y/y growth in Q2. As a refresher, the majority of Domino's revenue comes from its supply chain (selling raw ingredients to its franchisee restaurants), and supply chain revenue grew 7% y/y to $697 million, reflecting 61% of the company total.

Of course, all eyes are on the company's same-store sales growth (which reflects the sales growth in existing store locations, excluding the contribution of newly opened and newly closed stores). What stands out to me is the fact that U.S. same-store sales accelerated to 5.2% y/y growth, up 180bps sequentially versus 3.4% growth in Q2.

International slowed slightly, but largely held pace at 1.7% same-store sales growth versus 2.4% growth in Q2. What makes Domino's success stand out is the comparison versus other large restaurant chain peers.

Chipotle, whose poor results have cut the stock in nearly half relative to where it began in 2025, reported just 0.3% comparable sales growth (a slight improvement versus a -4.0% decline in Q2, but well below the 1.0% growth that the Street was expecting):

As a more direct competitor in the fast-food space, McDonald's (MCD) turned in 2.4% comps growth in the U.S. and 3.6% overall comp sales growth, with the U.S. weakening 10bps from 2.5% in Q2, and overall company weakening 20bps from 3.8%.

It's quite evident from Domino's acceleration to mid-single-digit comps while peers are showcasing flat to low single-digit growth that the company is gaining share. Like McDonald's, Domino's is focusing on winning customers through a spotlight on promotions and value items, and per management's commentary on the Q3 earnings call, this strategy is winning market share for Domino's:

It was a great Q3 for our U.S. business. We grew in all areas key to our success. Our carryout business was positive, our delivery business was positive, and our order count growth was positive. All of this resulted in meaningful market share growth [...]

Let's start with our Best Deal Ever promotion, which was a meaningful driver of our strong U.S. results in Q3. In my opinion, Best Deal Ever is well the best deal in restaurants. The price point screens renowned value and the taste drives our most delicious food perceptions. After all, consumers are building and eating their dream pizzas. In a world where prices have gone up and discounts never seem to be on the items you truly want, Domino's gives customers their favorite pizzas at our best price. Best Deal Ever also highlights the operational excellence our system has achieved."

Management notes as well that the success of recent promotions plus menu changes have improved profitability for franchisees, which is important for franchisees to be encouraged to continue growing their store footprints. The company notes that its relaunch of Bread Bites has been a big success for deal-oriented customers ($6.99 for 32 bread bites via a Mix and Match deal), while allowing franchisees to remove Bread Twists which were operationally more difficult to produce.

On the profitability front, Domino's operating income grew 12% y/y to $223.2 million, reflecting a 19.5% margin that improved 110bps y/y. The company benefited from a 70bps jump in gross margins in its supply chain business. The company also showed tremendous OPEX discipline, keeping general and administrative spending roughly flat y/y and reducing it by 50bps as a percentage of revenue.

Domino's free cash flow of $495.6 million in the first three quarters of FY25 also jumped at a rapid 32% y/y clip.

Valuation, risks, and key takeaways

Despite the fact that Domino's recent performance is outstripping its peers, Domino's now trades at a discount to most large-cap peers in the broader restaurant sector. As shown below, the stock's 17x EBITDA and 23x P/E multiples sit well below other fast-food and fast-casual peers:

Of course, there are also risks that we should keep in mind here. Domino's still maintains a substantial debt load at a 4.5x leverage ratio. We note as well that few food categories are as crowded as pizza, which will make it difficult for Domino's to distinguish itself outside of price promotions.

That said, I'm enthusiastic about Domino's prospects as its same-store sales growth races ahead of peers despite the stock's declining multiples. Take advantage of this dislocation as a buying opportunity.

data.title: The Honest Company: Reducing Complexity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The share price of The Honest Company (HNST) fell 25% to $2.49 following its 3Q 2025 results announcement. It was not just the huge revenue miss that caused investors to dump the stock but rather the announcement of yet another transformation plan (dubbed Transformation 2.0). Investors have endured a lot with HNST since its IPO and were looking forward to finally being on a growth trajectory (growing sales and expanding adjusted EBITDA margin).

I will elaborate more on it below, but the gist of Transformation 2.0 is focusing on higher margin products and channels. This means reducing product categories and sales channels, which will immediately lead to a drop in sales (the categories that company plans to drop accounted for 21% of 9M 2025 sales). However, the remaining product categories are both fast growing and high margin. Add to it that a simpler product portfolio should also lower SG&A costs. Thus, though it may seem that this action is bad for growth (reducing sales) it should be good for EBITDA (profitability). Accordingly, by having a much leaner product portfolio, HNST will be able to achieve more with less.

In my view, HNST is a high risk, high reward investment. The share price has corrected substantially and is now pricing a lot of decelerations in sales. However, if Transformation 2.0 achieves its objectives, the stock will rally on growth in profitability. The road ahead will be very bumpy. I expect 4Q 2025 to be another weak quarter, weighed down by the high restructuring costs the company guided for. It is likely that some of the restructuring costs will spill into 1Q 2026. As a result, 2Q 2026 is the earliest quarter that should begin to show meaningful improvement in adjusted EBITDA due to Transformation 2.0.

Transformation 2.0: Breaking the Numbers

On June 2, 2025, Curtiss Bruce became the new CFO of HNST, and only a few months later came the Transformation 2.0 announcement. That is unlikely to be a coincidence, as Transformation 2.0 appears to be more numbers driven than an operational turnaround. Transformation 2.0 is a simple pivot towards higher margin categories where HNST has demonstrated strength. This entails simplifying the product offering to focus on just wipes, personal care and diapers and exiting the apparel business. The second part of the plan is shutting down costly pathways to the consumers, which includes ending sales through Honest.com and exiting Canada. This plan signals that management will focus on categories and channels where the economics make sense.

In the 3Q 2025 earnings presentation the company introduced the term “organic revenue”, which points at the revenue from the categories and channels that it intends to retain. Based on 9M 2025 figures, organic revenue stands at $224 million, representing 79% of total revenue. Thus, the immediate impact of Transformation 2.0 is a 21% reduction in sales. The question is whether this contraction in sales could generate higher profits.

HNST generated a gross profit of $110 million in 9M 2025 that implies a gross profit margin (GPM) of 38.8%. If we assume that the discontinued categories and channels generate a GPM of 28.8% (10 percentage points lower than the company average), it implies that the organic categories have a GPM of 41.4%. Based on this assumption, it would mean that HNST would be losing only 15% of its gross profit as opposed to 21% of sales.

Building further on this assumption, a GPM of 41.4% should translate to an adjusted EBITDA margin of 9% for the organic business (as opposed to the group total adjusted EBITDA margin of 6.4% in 9M 2025). This means that the 9M 2025 adjusted EBITDA has scope to rerate upwards by around $2 million (or 11%), from $18.1 million to $20.2 million based on my calculations. This essentially highlights how HNST will likely be able to do more with less. The cherry on top being that those are the higher growth categories.

Figure 1 Estimating Adjusted EBITDA for the Organic Business

A Layered Guidance

During the 3Q 2025 earnings call, management provided guidance that tells two stories. First, the updated full year sales guidance was brought down significantly to a growth of -3% to 0% compared to growth of 4% to 6% from the previously issued guidance. This was a painful revision for investors. However, given that the company is ending several categories and channels in 4Q 2025, it means there must be a silver lining somewhere. That’s where the guidance for organic revenue comes into focus, as it still points towards a 4% to 6% growth. This part of the guidance means that management is still seeing momentum in sales in the categories that matter despite facing a weaker consumer environment. The lower guidance for adjusted EBITDA is not surprising if a company is winding down some parts of its business. It likely means that some segments will still incur costs as revenue drops for some time until the business is fully right sized.

Moreover, management announced during the call that due to Transformation 2.0 the company will incur a one-time cost of $25 million to $35 million. They did not elaborate on whether this will be taken in 4Q 2025 alone or if it will spillover into 1Q 2026. This will be a tough pill to swallow, but a necessary step in the company’s effort to reduce complexity.

Figure 2 Summary of FY 2025 Guidance

Balance Sheet Looks Capable of Absorbing Stress

The next two quarters will see HNST incur large losses arising from restructuring costs related to Transformation 2.0. It is a good thing that HNST has a solid balance sheet, whereby it has zero debt and $71 million in cash. This provides the buffer needed to absorb such a shock without needing to raise any capital, even at the higher end of restructuring costs forecast (and assuming all are cash costs). Thus, the cash-rich balance sheet gives management enough flexibility to execute the transformation plan.

Key Risks

This is the second transformation plan for HNST. There are no guarantees that this plan will work or that it will lead to better profitability. Moreover, it is impossible to rule out a third transformation plan by the end of next year. So, this is a very risky bet on a company that doesn't have a great track record for success. Another risk worth noting is the weak macro conditions facing consumers, which was heavily emphasized by management in the 3Q 2025 earnings call. Given that HNST products sell at more premium pricing, the company could face slower sales than anticipated. Consumers could downshift to save money, and this leaves the diapers category the most exposed.

Valuation

HNST trades at an EV-to-Sales (TTM) ratio of 0.57x based on a share price of $2.52, which is well below the consumer staples sector median of 1.49x. However, TTM sales level for HNST needs to be adjusted to match the organic revenue. Thus, by reducing the TTM sales by 20% (equivalent to the 9M2025 ratio of organic revenue to total revenue), we get an adjusted EV-to-Sales (TTM) ratio of 0.71x. This is still well below median for the consumer staples sector. This shows that the valuation has a lot of scope to rerate upwards if Transformation 2.0 proves to be a success.

Figure 3 Valuation Metrics

Final Thoughts

HNST is starting to think small in order to grow. HNST was behaving like a large consumer goods company by juggling multiple products, many of which were not getting traction in the market. Focusing on its strength in wipes will allow it to grow within that niche. Moreover, relying on Amazon for e-commerce makes strategic sense when the two main products sold are wipes and diapers. A simplified product line coupled with a focused go to market strategy should drive growth in profitability. This could be an interesting phase for HNST. I am assigning a Buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Marten Transport: One Of The Top Candidates In Trucking
data.description:
Marten Transport (MRTN) is a trucking company specialized in temperature-controlled transportation. Its share price has fallen heavily together with most of the stocks in the sector. There are many reasons behind the decline, such as a dauntingly long freight recession, low freight rates, higher costs, supply chain disruptions, and finally tariffs. As a result, many smaller companies have gone out of business, and trucking companies are reluctant to invest in new equipment.

Marten appears to be one of the safest companies to invest in for the potential turnaround in the freight market. So far Marten’s business has remained profitable, it has large insider ownership, and most importantly, it has a debt-free balance sheet. Marten also pays a modest dividend, and the dividend yield has climbed to an unusually high level.

A trucking company with a focus

Marten has four different businesses: Truckload, Dedicated, Brokerage and MRTN de Mexico. Truckload is its largest business, bringing in 48% of the revenue, followed by Dedicated with 30% share of revenue. Approximately 60% of Truckload and Dedicated revenue is from temperature sensitive deliveries. At the end of 2024, Marten had 3000 company and contractor tractors, 5400 trailers, and 3800 employees. Marten has a market cap and enterprise value of approximately $800 and $750 million.

In the midst of numerous headwinds, Marten’s revenues have decreased for ten quarters in a row. The year 2024 ended with a 15% revenue decline and a 63% drop in operating income. In the first nine months of 2025, Marten’s revenue clocked in at $673 million compared to $733 in the comparison period, a decline of 8%. In the third quarter the decline was approximately 7%. Operating income dropped 31% to $18.3 million in the first nine months.

A top candidate in the industry

Hopes of freight market recovery have been pushed forward many times over. The recent industry reports still give a mixed picture. According to ACT Research, freight market has moved from sharp contraction to extended correction. Volumes are still soft, but spot rates show signs of a slight increase, and driver availability is in balance. The trucking capacity is adjusting to the current market situation, low volumes and prices, in two main ways. Existing trucking companies are exiting the industry as costs have risen much faster than freight rates. Secondly, sales of new tractors are below the required rate to maintain current fleets.

Marten has responded to the difficult market situation in multiple ways. It has cut capital expenditures heavily. Typically it invests $120-150 million, but this year Marten estimates spending $64 million. Marten maintains a young fleet of tractors. In 2024 the average age was only 1.9 years, and it typically replaces them 4 years after purchase. Unfortunately, the prices of tractors and refrigerated trailers have increased significantly, 17% and 30% compared to 2021, which showcases the level of cost inflation trucking companies have faced.

Focusing on temperature-controlled transportation exposes Marten to more stable end markets such as food and beverage. Perhaps surprisingly, the focus has not protected it from a larger revenue fall compared to many of its peers, such as Covenant Logistics (CVLG), Knight-Swift (KNX) and Werner Enterprises (WERN), which have posted better performing toplines. In response to lower volumes, Marten has increased its customer base by adding 70-100 new customers per year for the past three years, which could increase its earnings power if volumes and rates recover. Still, around half of Marten’s revenue comes from the top 10 customers, posing a risk from potential customer loss.

Recently, Marten appointed Randolph Marten as a CEO to replace retiring Timothy Kohl, who served as president of the company for 17 years. The replacement is also a Marten veteran and a former CEO between 2005 and 2021. From this perspective, the company should be in safe hands, but it is worrisome if the company cannot raise new suitable candidates for the position. Randolph Marten owns 21.8% of the company shares, aligning his work rather well with the shareholders.

In September, Marten sold its intermodal assets, including 1200 refrigerated containers, to Hub Group (HUBG) for $52 million. Intermodal business represented only 4% of Marten’s revenue. The sale strengthens Marten’s balance sheet and increases Marten’s focus on truck transportation. On the other hand, it narrows Marten’s offering in refrigerated transportation. In the first nine months, the revenue of Intermodal decreased 26%, so the sale should help Marten to report better comparison figures in the coming quarters.

Marten has a strong balance sheet to endure the difficult times. It has no long-term debt. Thanks to the sale, Marten’s cash position increased to $50 million in Q3 compared to $17 million at the year's end. Its strong financial position will help the company to have a full focus on business development and operational efficiency.

Attractively valued, but big upside is not yet visible

As Marten’s shares are still in a clear downtrend, the earnings estimates have also been heading lower recently. In the past months, earnings estimates have been revised down from $0.3 to $0.2 per share, to a level last seen in 2009-2010. So far it appears that estimates have dropped even faster than the share price. There are three analysts following Marten with an average target price of $11. Also, the target price has been revised significantly lower in the past few months.

In the past ten years, Marten’s earnings per share have averaged $0.77. When excluding the boom years of 2021 and 2022, when earnings reached $1-1.35 per share, the average still stands at $0.67. Analysts estimate Marten to reach EPS of $0.4 in 2027, which indicates expectations are rather low for the coming years.

Key questions are what will be the level of earnings in a more normal market environment, what is an acceptable valuation for Marten, and when a market turnaround happens. For the first two, we can try to make estimates based on history, while the last one likely remains the biggest question mark.

Over the very long term, 20 years, Marten’s net income margin has averaged approximately 5.5%. Over the past decade, the average margin has averaged 7%. If we assume that Marten’s revenue grows to one billion, higher than in 2024 but significantly lower than 2022 and 2023, and a net income margin of 6%, the earnings per share would reach $0.74. The P/E multiple averaged 17.4x during 2019-2022, while net income margin averaged 7.8%.

If we apply a P/E multiple of 15x, we could see a share price of $11.1, which is not meaningfully higher than the share price today. An investor needs to expect that Marten’s shares will be valued higher and/or the profitability will inflect higher, which might be difficult to achieve due to the cost inflation.

On the surface, Marten appears to be valued in line with its peer group. Considering the qualitative factors, especially the balance sheet, and profitability, Marten fares well. Most of its peers are valued higher, posting lower profitability and carrying more debt. Over the past decade, Marten’s EV/EBITDA has averaged 6.7x and P/B 2x. The discount to average multiples is well explained by the current business trajectory and profitability.

Ticker
