Dividend yield has increased to a decent level

Since 2022, Marten has paid an annual dividend of $0.24, translating to a modest yield of 2.4%. In the boom years of 2019-2021, Marten rewarded shareholders with significant special dividends, demonstrating prudent capital allocation when many other companies acquired or invested in more capacity.

At the moment all earnings are consumed by the dividend, which creates a risk of a dividend cut, although the $20 million annual dividend can be paid from the cash reserves. Marten has paid a dividend since 2010, so breaking the streak likely takes even more severe market conditions to push the company into a dividend cut.

Conclusion

The freight recession is unlikely over, and stocks in the sector are in a downtrend. Things might still get worse before turning to better. A cautious investor should wait for an even lower purchase price, although some upside is visible as the market works itself towards a better position for the trucking companies. Marten’s qualitative factors make it a top candidate in the industry, the valuation is low historically and in relation to key peers. However, both revenue and profitability need to increase significantly in order to see upside.

data.title: BorgWarner: Goldilocks EV Positioning With Nearly 47% Upside
data.description:
The long-term trend remains intact, but the electric vehicle adoption curve is in a fragmented landscape. Norway is already in the late majority stage, as over 98% of its new cars in recent months were EVs. China is well into the early majority, with more than half of its 2025 new car sales expected to be electric. Europe is transitioning into that phase, whereas the US, Canada, and other countries are still at the early adopter point. Whether by design or good fortune, BorgWarner’s (BWA) timing for this transition seems to be balanced and optimal. This is important as it allows the company to capture cash flows in the mature ICE (or, Internal Combustion Engine) market and growth in the emerging market without overexposure to any single phase of adoption. A sum-of-the-parts valuation that draws on this positioning shows a roughly 47% upside. Given the favorable risk-reward profile, I am initiating BorgWarner with a “Strong Buy” rating.

A Leaner Portfolio With a Clear Bet on Hybrids and China

A thing I like here is how BWA is chopping and changing its portfolio proactively and is staying sharply focused on high-margin, high-barrier propulsion systems rather than low-value parts. In 2023, the company spun off the Fuel Systems segment into PHINIA (PHIN) as a part of its “Charging Forward” strategy to shed ICE exposure. And since 2020, it has been acquiring companies like Delphi, AKASOL, and Drivetek to increase exposure to electrification. In 2Q25 the company also exited the charging business, which was becoming commoditized, and management did not see a path to a scale where it could earn a 15%+ return on invested capital.

The company’s portfolio as it stands now is divided into two types, which it calls Foundational products and eProducts.

Segments Type Products 2024 % of revenue
Turbos & Thermal Technologies Foundational Variable turbine geometry (VTG) turbochargers , eBoosters, and advanced thermal management systems, such as high-voltage coolant heaters, that are used across all propulsion types, including hybrids and BEVs. 41.79%
Drivetrain & Morse Systems Foundational Engine timing systems (chains, tensioners), on-demand (TOD) transfer cases, and advanced all-wheel-drive systems like cross-wheel drives (XWD). 39.59%
PowerDrive Systems eProducts Components for electric propulsion, including inverters , e-motors , and highly integrated drive modules (iDM). 13.75%
Battery & Charging Systems eProducts Historically focused on battery management systems but is being restructured. 5.18%

At first glance, it might look like the company’s core segments are slowly losing relevance, but I think this needs more nuance. Automakers are increasingly using advanced turbochargers to achieve V6 power from a 4-cylinder hybrid engine. This shift uses less fuel while staying within the tighter emission standards of modern regulations. To meet the same standards, these high-efficiency engines also need Exhaust Gas Recirculation systems. And BWA is a market leader in these exact components.

Market researchers have forecasted ambitious future forecasts for automotive turbochargers. But I am more cautious about those numbers. I think the picture is getting murkier as increasing turbocharger use (including e-turbochargers in hybrids) is offset by BEV adoption rates, which do not require turbochargers.

The Drivetrain segment is also a major reason why the company’s revenue opportunity on a hybrid is four times that of a gas engine. The segment’s major customers are truck and SUV makers, and this category is moving from gas-only to hybrid first. An advanced hybrid needs clutch systems and torsional dampers to smoothly blend power from the gas engine and electric motor. This makes the Drivetrain segment a critical enabler of the hybrid boom.

If the Drivetrain segment supplies mechanical parts to blend power, the PowerDrive segment supplies the electric propulsion to be blended. These are the motors and inverters that add electric torque and effectively position the company on both sides of the powertrain. The segment, though, is a source of worry for investors as it is a capital-intensive business and a significant drag on profitability. So, a question I asked was – at the expense of investments, is there any strategic value being built here?

In April this year, the Financial Times did a great story on how the tide had turned and it was now the Europeans who were learning from innovations in Chinese EVs. It is becoming increasingly clear that to be on the right side of the EV race, auto part makers need to have exposure to the Chinese EV market.

Raymond Tsang, an automotive technology expert with Bain in Shanghai, says after losing a third of their market share in China since 2020, foreign automakers simply “have no choice” but to partner with Chinese technology companies to have even a chance of survival. - Financial Times

BWA isn’t among the Western suppliers being displaced by domestic competition in China. It has become a key technology partner to Chinese OEMs, and recent announcements confirm this.

The data is also striking: 90% of the exposure in the Chinese business is to domestic Chinese OEMs. The way I look at it, the foundational business is benefitting from hybrids in the West and eProducts from the high-volume growth in China.

SOTP Valuation

I have used a sum-of-the-parts valuation, as it is the only way to untangle the two distinct businesses within BWA. The foundational segments continue to generate strong margins and steady cash flows, though sales have flattened or are in decline. These are best valued using an EV/AOI (Adjusted Operating Income) multiple, as profitability, not growth, is the main driver of value for these segments.

The eProduct portfolio operates at a loss, requires significant investments, but is expanding at a 20%+ rate. An EV/sales multiple for these segments better reflects how the market values early-stage growth assets. To anchor the valuation, I’ve identified direct peers for each segment and calibrated the valuation multiples to reflect differences in margin profile and growth potential.

For instance, Garrett Motion (GTX) is a turbocharger specialist and is the most direct publicly traded peer for the Turbo segment. GTX reported its 3Q earnings last month and guided FY25 AOI at $510 million for an EV/AOI multiple of 9.25x. GTX’s AOI margins also hover around 14% - 15% compared to 11% for BWA’s turbo segment. So, I applied a discounted multiple of 7.0x here.

With the Drivetrain segment, Dana Incorporated (DAN) is the most directly comparable in terms of product, but its operating income is too low to be useful. I have therefore anchored it to a range of comparable companies. Allison Transmission (ALSN) is an outlier in this group, as with its recent sales decline, the market sees it as very vulnerable to EV trends. I have persisted with the average multiple in the peer group for this segment even though it should ideally get a higher multiple due to superior margins and a favorable hybrid exposure.

The eProduct side is harder to benchmark because there are few companies with a similar mix of electric drive modules and power electronics. A 1.25 times sales multiple fits both the growth profile and the capital needs of the business. And since management is exiting the charging unit, I have assigned no value to that segment for now.

Risks to the thesis

In 2023, BWA had outlined a very ambitious goal of achieving 2027 eProducts category sales of $10 billion at an adjusted operating margin of 7%. Contrast that to the reality of 2025; sales may be just shy of $3 billion at the end of the year. More importantly, the operating margins in these segments for the first nine months were -7%. So, to hit those targets, the company needs to expand margins by 14% while increasing sales by 3.33 times. I think these targets are already in jeopardy. Operating leverage can help margins, but I am skeptical of a 3.33x revenue growth on a 2-year timeline. I have therefore not assumed these heroic targets in my calculations. Additionally, if the EV margin story is structurally lower than management expects, multiples will compress.

My bull thesis also relies on a Goldilocks exposure to the EV transition, which means the foundational segment remains high-margin cash cows until the transition is complete. The strategic narrative would break if the foundational ICE business melts faster than eProducts can scale.

Conclusion

BorgWarner is not a simple EV bet or a simple ICE cash cow. It sits in the middle of a global transition where different markets are moving at different speeds, and that positioning gives the company room to earn today while building for tomorrow. The hybrid boom in North America and Europe keeps the foundational business relevant, while strong ties to Chinese EV makers give the eProducts portfolio a real path to scale. The valuation still reflects investor skepticism, which leaves upside for anyone willing to look past the near-term noise and focus on the company’s balanced exposure, disciplined portfolio choices, and improving growth mix. To me, that combination supports a constructive view and makes the current setup attractive for long-term investors.

data.title: The Market Dislikes CleanSpark's Convertible Notes (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
It has been over six months since I last covered CleanSpark (CLSK) for Seeking Alpha. At that time, the stock was trading at about $8 per share, and I called it a speculative 'buy' based on a technical breakout in the price of Bitcoin (BTC-USD) that I thought would propel CLSK shares higher.

While I've certainly had some Bitcoin miner calls that have not worked out as well this year, the CLSK 'buy' was well-timed. The stock rallied through spring and early summer before pulling back just before a monster run in September and early October that took shares of CLSK up north of $20 per share. It's been a rough go for CLSK shareholders since.

In this update, we'll look at the company's recently announced convertible note offering and build a book value estimate based on the company's mining footprint and BTC holdings.

$1.15 Billion Convertible Notes

CleanSpark had long been one of the few Bitcoin miners that held off on the HPC/AI data center pivot. That recently changed. And while I'm not sure I'm the biggest fan of pivoting to HPC this late in the game, if CleanSpark is going to actually enter this business, it's doing so with a capital raise that I think is reasonably advantageous to the company.

On November 10th, CleanSpark announced $1 billion in zero-interest convertible notes due in February 2032. That offering was up-sized to $1.15 billion shortly after. Per the terms of deal, the convertible notes are unsecured and are convertible to either cash, common at $19.16 per share, or some combination of the two at CleanSpark's discretion. Following the capital raise, CleanSpark spent $460 million of the proceeds and bought back 30.6 million shares at $15.03 per share. It's an interesting strategy because it cuts the potential shareholder dilution from converts by slightly more than half:

Value Share Price Shares
Converts $1,150,000,000 $19.16 60,020,877
Buybacks $460,000,000 $15.03 30,605,456
Net dilution 29,415,421

Source: CleanSpark, analyst's calculations

So essentially, the company has raised roughly $690 million that it plans to invest in its HPC buildout. The net dilution of 29.4 million shares represents an increase of about 10% from the 281 million shares outstanding at the end of June. The market's reaction to converts has been quite poor, to say the least:

With Bitcoin down about 3% since CleanSpark's convertible note announcement on the 10th, shares of CLSK are down more than 26%. And while the mining sector is broadly down, CLSK has been beaten up a bit more than peers like Hut 8 (HUT) or MARA Holdings (MARA); each of which are similarly sized by market capitalization.

Valuation Considerations

With the caveat that we don't yet have calendar Q3 numbers for CleanSpark, given what we know about CleanSpark's convertible note details, we can build a rough estimate for the company's book valuation today based on the same 50 EH/s in operational hashrate that CleanSpark had at the end of Q2.

Valuation Estimate Figures
Bitcoin Holdings $1,251,168,000
Net Prop/Plant/Equip $1,350,000,000
Goodwill/Other assets $550,000,000
Post-Convert Cash $690,000,000
Total Assets $3,841,168,000
Total Liabilities End of Q2 $955,000,000
Convert Debt $1,150,000,000
Shareholder equity $1,736,168,000
Shares Outstanding 251,000,000
Book value $6.92

Source: Analyst's Estimate based on Q2 balance sheet, BTC value, and

In this table, I've marked the company's 13,033 BTC to a market price of $96,000, which is the level that Bitcoin is currently trading at as I write this. I've also reduced the company's shares outstanding from Q2 by 30 million, given CleanSpark's recent share buyback. Given the updated shares and Bitcoin price, I think we can reasonably assume CLSK shares have a book valuation around $7 following the convertible note offering. At an intraday CLSK price of $11.50, I estimate CLSK is trading at a P/B ratio between 1.6 and 1.7. This is not a historically high P/B valuation multiple for CLSK shares, but it isn't necessarily cheap either:

CleanSpark stock often experiences wild swings in price over very short periods of time. If we go back to last year, a price-to-book level between 1 and 1.2 has been a much better place to buy CLSK shares for a price bounce. However, it absolutely must be noted that during 'crypto winter,' CLSK traded at deep discounts to its book value. At one point in December 2021, CLSK traded at a price-to-book level of just 0.25. My point is, playing CLSK for a swing trade bounce at these levels may come down to where you think we are in Bitcoin's cycle. And unfortunately, I don't think we're at the bottom yet.

Closing Summary

I have made it no mystery that I've been highly cautious about Bitcoin's price setup for several months. If Bitcoin continues to decline in price, CLSK will almost certainly go down in sympathy. Despite the fact that the company is looking to branch out into HPC and AI compute, the fact of the matter is almost all of the company's assets are tied to Bitcoin's value. As of writing, Bitcoin's hash price has fallen under $40 PH/S per day. The economics of mining continue to be a major headwind for companies like CleanSpark, which I suspect is why mining peer Bitfarms (BITF) is positioning to exit the business entirely.

I still think CLSK can serve as a terrific swing trade vehicle in the right situation. One of the things that I like to see when picking higher conviction swing trades in the mining stocks is a convergence of factors that I view as indicative of a short-to-medium-term bottom in price. Those factors include valuation compression, reaction to bad news or short squeeze potential, and a positive technical setup for BTC. Right now, I only see one of those factors for CLSK given the market's reaction to the convertible notes. And while I think the market reaction to CleanSpark's convertible note offering was probably a little bit overdone, history says valuation can compress even more. I don't think we're at the bottom for BTC yet. Thus, I'd wait before trying to catch the knife in CLSK.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Is Getting A Bit Pricey (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In the chart below, you can see that I have been bullish on The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) this year, and that seems to have paid off. The stock is up another ~13% since the reiteration of my buy rating back in September. Back in their Q2 results, I found accelerating growth on the top and bottom line and a strong rebound in their investment banking business. The company reported earnings in mid-October and so we'll see if I need to reassess my rating on the stock now.

Below, it is shown that Goldman Sachs' fundamentals are rock solid. Net revenue growth continues to accelerate, and the company's flagship investment banking unit is roaring back to life. While the profitability of the firm is also impressive, the valuation has gotten a bit pricey in my view. With their P/B now around 90% above the sector median, it seems that a lot of good news has been baked into the stock price. With the risk/reward now only near neutral, I have decided to lower my rating to a hold. Investors should wait for a better entry point into this top-notch big bank.

Growth Accelerates Further

Let's start with an overview of their top line. As you can see, for 2025 Q3, Goldman Sachs generated a total of $15.184 billion in revenues, and that represents a good growth rate of 20% YoY. In fact, it's better than just good, as a 15% top-line increase was reported in the previous quarter, and so it seems that overall business activity continues to pick up at the company. We'll discuss the segment earnings below, but you can see above that all segments saw significant growth, even their Platform Solutions. In terms of performance versus expectations, Goldman Sachs beat on revenue by an impressive margin of $933.59 million. As a whole, I would say top-line results are favorable as they continue the trend of top line growth acceleration, and that's, of course, a good sign.

Global Banking & Markets

Global Banking & Markets is by far their largest segment by revenues, and so it's worth spending some time looking at these results. For the segment, net revenues were up by 18% YoY, and while that's less explosive growth that Q2's 24% increase, this Q3 growth rate is still highly respectable.

Earlier, I stated that an investment banking rebound was shown in their Q2 results. In Q3, there are further signs that this key business is rebounding for Goldman Sachs. Investment banking fees soared 42% YoY to $2.657 billion and led the way in terms of growth within this segment. The company remains one of the largest underwriters on Wall Street and so the importance of this sharp increase in activity cannot be overstated. Goldman Sachs stated that they saw "higher net revenues in Advisory and Debt underwriting and higher net revenues in Equity underwriting" and so it seems that overall capital markets activity is bouncing back significantly.

As for their trading business, FICC saw 17% growth, while Equities saw 7% growth. The FICC growth rate is quite robust, and while the Equities growth rate is a bit sluggish, the summer months didn't have a great deal of volatility, and so modest growth isn't particularly concerning. The main risk regarding this segment moving forward is the fact that the U.S. Government shutdown has caused many IPOs to be delayed. Therefore, in Q4, investment banking fees may be weighed on. Still, with the overall investment banking sector showing good signs of recovery, the longer-term outlook has become bright.

Asset & Wealth Management

For their Asset & Wealth Management segment, Goldman Sachs also showed favorable results. Firstly, net revenues also increased by 17% to $4.399 billion. Management and other fees saw respectable growth of 12%, driven by higher average AUS. For private banking and lending, there was a 40% surge in revenues, but that was mostly because of interest received from a previously impaired loan. Lastly, for their debt investments, they had net markups.

Better insight into how this segment really performed can be derived from the metrics shown above. As you can see, total AUS increased notably YoY and QoQ to $3.452 trillion. What's even more encouraging is the fact that net inflows of $79 billion are much stronger than in the prior year period. Therefore, clients continue to place their trust into Goldman Sachs regarding the management of their wealth. This investor confidence is for sure a good sign. While net market appreciation was a bit lower than back in the year ago quarter to show less robust investment performance, $80 billion of appreciation is still overall a decent figure. As a whole, this segment showed strong results as both revenues and AUS signaled positive business momentum.

Platform Solutions

Like in my previous articles, I will just very briefly mention their Platform Solutions results, as they are basically now exiting this business. For this segment, net revenues increased by a whopping 71% YoY with strength in both Consumer platforms and in Transaction banking & other. However, Goldman Sachs stated that in the prior year period, they had a net loss in the former due to the General Motors (GM) credit card program and had markdowns in the latter related to their seller financing loan portfolio, and so the large increase in revenues for this segment is overall inflated by non-operating factors. They reported that the sale of the General Motors credit card business is now complete.

ROE & ROTE Improve Sharply

Booming business activity is one thing. Returns are another. However, Goldman Sachs has shown great improvements on both. In their Q3, the company reported that operating expenses were up 14% YoY to $9.453 billion. While that is still quite a sum, note that the increase in expenses trailed the top line growth rate as an indication that the bank is getting run in an efficient manner. Another contributor to profitability was the lower provision for credit losses. There was a 15% decrease YoY to $339 million, and so it seems that Goldman Sachs is starting to be more confident regarding the macro-environment.

For the bottom line, they reported $12.25, up 46% YoY, and with this growth rate being much better than Q2's 27%, I believe it is fair to say that their business is firing on all cylinders right now. Lastly, the profitability ratios are well worth mentioning here. In the year-ago quarter, annualized ROE was reported to be 10.4%, while ROTE was 11.1%. Fast forward one year, in this year's Q3, annualized ROE was 14.2%, while ROTE was 15.2%. This is a major step up in terms of capital efficiency, and so investors are likely very pleased.

Valuation Getting A Little Pricey

Goldman Sachs' fundamentals have been improving, but its stock's valuation has also been on a rather consistent climb upwards. As of now, the P/B ratio stands at 2.30 which is a multiyear high. At this valuation, there should be a degree of cautiousness in my view.

Yes, Goldman Sachs is seeing continued top line growth acceleration. Yes, they are seeing a strong rebound in investment banking activity. And yes, their profitability is looking very good right now. However, considering the fact that their P/B ratio is shown to be at a ~90% premium to the Financials sector, I would say that a lot of this good news is already getting priced in.

For a company with such robust fundamentals, I would say that a premium is definitely deserved, but with the valuation gap to the sector now being rather large, I would say the risk/reward may only be minimally attractive at current levels.

Concluding Remarks

Don't get me wrong. I'm incredibly optimistic about Goldman Sachs' business moving forward. While the government shutdown may be a near-term headwind to investment banking, the return of dealmaking is now obvious, and so great quarters are likely ahead for the company. As stated above, top and bottom line strength was impressive, and profitability ratio improvement has been encouraging. However, the valuation has become a potential deterrent in my view. Goldman Sachs is a top-notch big bank that deserves to trade at a premium, but despite their fundamental robustness, the premium seems to have gotten a bit out of hand. Therefore, I'm downgrading the stock from buy to hold but remain optimistic about the company itself.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Q3 Earnings Support Continued Resilience
data.description:
Overview

As the business development sector continues to face headwinds, it has revealed the strengths and weaknesses of the sector. While many other BDCs struggle to generate positive earnings growth and support payouts, others remain more resilient. I believe that Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) is one of the stronger choices in the sector, which is supported by its low non-accrual rate, consistent earnings, and management's ability to continue allocating capital towards new investment opportunities. When I previously covered BXSL, I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation at the time. Since BXSL has recently reported its Q3 earnings, I wanted to revisit BXSL's performance and outlook for the remainder of the year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that BXSL's share price has declined by a little over 14%. The share price has maintained a consistent decline over the last few quarters. Even when including all distributions that were paid, the total return still sits at a loss of about 5.3% over the same period. BXSL now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 11.5%, which makes it a great option for investors seeking a source of high-income generation.

Due to the recent pullback in its share price, BXSL continues to trade at an attractive discount to NAV valuation. For long-term investors, I believe that this presents a great time to accumulate shares. While there are different sources of uncertainty that can slow earnings growth over the next few quarters, I believe that BXSL has one of the strongest fundamentals across the BDC sector. So let's start by taking a look at the most recent earnings report and assessing some of the risks.

Q3 Earnings & Risk Profile

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, BXSL has an investment fair value of $13.8B that is spread across 311 different portfolio companies. Unlike traditional equities, BXSL generates its earnings by lending capital to middle-market borrowers and collects interest income in exchange. When assessing its portfolio, BXSL leans most heavily on first lien senior secured debt to generate its earnings. This is ideal because first lien debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment. This protects BXSL from losses in a scenario where a portfolio company defaults and needs to liquidate assets. The portfolio's breakdown by investment is as follows:

  • First Lien Debt: 97.5%
  • Second Lien Debt: 1.7%
  • Equity: 0.7%
  • Unsecured Debt: 0.1%

Another aspect of the portfolio's strength is the diversity across industries. As we can see below, BXSL is most heavily allocated to Software investments at 20% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Health Care Providers and Professional Services, which both account for 10% of assets respectively. The combination of these top three industries makes up 40% of BXSL's total portfolio, so investors should anticipate that these industries will have the greatest impact on BXSL's daily price movement and total return potential.

BXSL reported its Q3 earnings this week, and the results continue to demonstrate strength. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.82 per share, which beat expectations by $0.04 per share. While the net investment income was an improvement from the prior quarter's NII of $0.77 per share, it was also a decline from the prior year's net investment income of $0.91 per share. Interest income amounted to $328M for the quarter, which is a slight increase from the prior year's interest income of $321M.

However, the slight decline in net investment income can be attributed to the rising operating expenses for the quarter. For instance, interest expenses landed at $95M, which was an increase from the prior year's interest expense of $88M. Similarly, BXSL's management fee increased to $35M for the quarter, bringing the total operating expenses up to $165M for the quarter.

Fundamentally, I think that BXSL has demonstrated a healthy balance sheet and consistent earnings. The main issue that I have is the growing reliance on debt to fund new opportunities. For instance, BXSL now has $250M in cash and equivalents on hand. However, the total debt has now increased to $7.6B, which is an increase from the prior year's total debt of $6.38B. As a result, BXSL's debt-to-equity ratio has increased to 1.22x during the quarter, which is a sizeable increase from the prior year's debt-to-equity ratio of 1.12x.

From a risk perspective, BXSL's portfolio continues to have one of the lowest non-accrual rates across the BDC sector. Non-accruals measure the rate of portfolio companies that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance and aren't actively contributing towards a BDC's earnings. On the latest earnings call, management confirmed that non-accruals sit at 0.1% of fair portfolio value, which is extremely low compared to some of the most notable peers in the sector.

Our nonaccrual rates 0.1% at cost compares favorably to 2.9% for peers. Our PIKas a percentage of total investment income at 8.2% compares favorably to 11.3% for peers and our stressed debt investments marked below 80 at cost is approximately 0.9% compared to 4.1% for the peer average. - Jonathan Bock, Co-CEO

While non-accruals are only a piece of the entire picture, they can be a good indication of how strong management's underwriting and vulnerability to specific sectors can be. Just as a point of reference, here are some notable non-accrual rates for some of the most popular BDCs:

  • Hercules Capital (HTGC): 1.1% non-accrual rate of fair portfolio value.
  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 1% non-accrual rate of fair portfolio value.
  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC): 1.3% non-accrual rate of fair portfolio value.

Therefore, BXSL seems to be one of the most resilient BDCs across the sector at this time. I suspect that the overall structure of its portfolio is a large contributor to its resilience. For instance, BXSL is most heavily allocated to software investments, which typically have lower operating costs, and the business is more resilient against things like tariffs. So BXSL's suppressed share price may have more to do with the overall sentiment of the debt markets right now rather than some internal weakness within BXSL's portfolio.

Valuation

Since BXSL is structured as a business development company, the price can trade at a difference from the underlying value of its assets. Following the continuous decline, BXSL now trades at a discount to NAV of approximately 1.84%. For reference, BXSL has traded at an average premium to NAV of 6.7% over the last five year period. The red line on the chart below represents the current discount to NAV valuation in relation to its historical range. While this discount to NAV pricing could indicate an attractive time to accumulate, there are also some things to remain cautious about in the near term.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $30.41 per share, which indicates a potential upside of about 13.4% from its current level. I do believe that this double-digit upside is possible, assuming that management continues to allocate sufficient capital towards new investment activity. For instance, management was able to allocate a little over $1B towards new investment funding over the quarter. This was offset by approximately $437M in sales and repayments during the quarter, which resulted in a positive net increase in operations. Despite this, the larger level of liabilities has resulted in a slight NAV decline year over year:

  • Q3 2024: $27.27 per share
  • Q3 2025: $27.15 per share

The Fed has initiated its second 25 basis point cut to rates, which can improve the debt markets. Assuming that interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months, this may have a negative impact on net investment income in the short-term, but I believe this will be great in the long-term. As the cost of debt decreases, there may be an influx of new borrowers in the market, which may provide management with new investment opportunities that can increase earnings.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.77 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.5%. As previously mentioned, net investment income amounted to $0.82 per share during the quarter. This indicates that earnings support the distribution at a coverage rate of approximately 106%. While it's great that earnings continue to support the payouts, the thinning coverage warrants caution for investors seeking a source of reliable dividend income.

However, management also confirmed there to be about $1.89 per share worth of undistributed spillover income that can be tapped into. Additionally, management continues to invest sufficient capital into new growth opportunities, which could lead to higher earnings growth in the next few quarters. This strong portfolio quality and growth potential should alleviate some stress for investors worried about the possibility of a large reduction in income generation.

Lastly, investors should be aware that the distributions received from BXSL are not tax efficient. Since payouts are fueled by net investment income, the distributions can be classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. If you are an investor that wants to limit the tax liability, a position in BXSL would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on BXSL at this time due to the attractive valuation, consistent earnings, and management's ability to continue allocating capital towards new investment opportunities. Additionally, BXSL has one of the lowest non-accrual rates across the entire BDC sector, which reinforces the portfolio's continued strength during this higher interest rate environment. As management continues to allocate capital towards new investment opportunities, BXSL may continue to grow earnings when the macroenvironment becomes more favorable as a result of lower interest rates.

data.title: Topgolf Callaway Brands' Catalyst Offers More Upside
data.description:
November 14th was a hole in one for shareholders of Topgolf Callaway Brands (MODG). Shares of the company closed up 6.5% after news broke that the firm is apparently in discussions to sell its Topgolf unit to a private equity firm by the name of Leonard Green. The Wall Street Journal reported that the business should bring in somewhere around $1 billion. There is some uncertainty about this transaction, and it very well could come to naught. But what we do know is that Topgolf Callaway Brands is incredibly interested in parting with this enterprise after purchasing it, a couple of years ago, only to see its share price plummet. Originally, the plan was to split Topgolf and Callaway up into two separate companies once again. But because of a change in leadership, that idea ultimately fell through. And last month, rumors began circulating that the enterprise might actually consider selling off its Callaway operation in order to focus on its Topgolf business.

In my last article about the company, published in April of this year, I decided to reaffirm the firm as a 'strong buy' candidate. Even though the business had come under pressure in some respects, shares looked too cheap to ignore. In fact, even though I run a hyper-concentrated portfolio, I even picked up units of the business. And honestly, that has gone incredibly well for me. In fact, in the time that I have owned shares of the business, I am up 53%. And even though it might be tempting for some investors to sell out after seeing this move higher, I believe that there is more upside to be had. Because of this, I don't have an issue maintaining it as a 'strong buy' for now.

Shares are incredibly cheap

Although shares of Topgolf Callaway Brands are nowhere near as cheap as they were earlier this year, they are still very attractive, as I will detail shortly. But before we get to that, I think a discussion over the most recent financial performance of the business is in order. In my last article about the company, I detailed results through 2024. But since then, we have had three separate operating quarters worth of results. To start with, in the chart above, you can see financial results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Relative to 2024, things are not looking as good as I would like them to.

Revenue actually dropped from $1.01 billion to $934 million. This decline brought with it lower profitability as well. Net income went from negative $3.6 million to negative $14.7 million. Although operating cash flow jumped from $187.7 million to $228.4 million, it was an obvious outlier. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $55.4 million to $50.8 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business contracted from $119 million to $114.6 million.

When we look at the two different sets of operations here, we see some interesting results. For a while now, the firm's Topgolf venues had continued to add to the top line, though that growth was somewhat deceptive. Same-venue sales in 2024, for instance, were down 9%. I believe that this is primarily the result of weaker economic conditions, with consumers coming under pressure because of high-interest rates and inflationary pressures aimed at combating them. But this year, things are a little different.

Same venue sales in the most recent quarter grew by 1%. I know that's not much, but it's a heck of a lot better than a decline. Management said that this was because of improved traffic trends from certain value initiatives that the company had been implementing. Management gave a couple of examples here, such as 50% off golf during certain times of the week, as well as the Sunday Fun Pass that management claimed they sold twice as many of compared to a year earlier. A growth in venue count also helped here, with that number expanding from 98 last year to 102 this year. The firm also saw a rise in the number of international franchise venues that it gets royalties from increase from five to eight. As revenue for the company improved, profitability improved also, with a rise in bottom line results from $28.3 million last year to $31.1 million this year. This actually marks the start of a turnaround, considering that, for the first nine months of this year, the $74.6 million in profits generated by this unit ended up being down from the $87.3 million reported a year earlier.

There technically are two other operating segments that the company has. One of these is the Golf Equipment segment, while the other is the Active Lifestyle segment. But honestly, I don't think it would make sense to analyze each of these in turn. I say this because it is almost guaranteed at this point that, whether through an asset sale of one business or the other or a return to the idea of a spin-off, Topgolf and Callaway are two distinct companies that should be looked at separately. So instead of looking at the Golf Equipment and Active Lifestyle segments separately, I am combining them under the umbrella of the Callaway name.

Revenue for these operations declined from $559.7 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $461.8 million in the third quarter of 2025. As disappointing as this appears to be at first glance, we need to keep in mind that, earlier this year, management sold off its Jack Wolfskin brand in a deal valued at $290 million. This was a part of the business that generated practically no profitability and, in some years, even generated losses. But it did generate a lot in the way of revenue. And this change alone negatively affected top line results by $108 million. Excluding this, revenue would have risen slightly from $451.5 million to $461.6 million. It's also worth noting that while organic revenue was up, profitability was still down, with a decline from $46.2 million to $36.9 million. Management blamed this was primarily because of tariffs. In fact, tariffs negatively affected results by $5.8 million during this time. However, there were other factors also, such as a $2.2 million hit that the company took from the sale of its Jack Wolfskin business that was completed in May of this year.

One thing that I'm certainly happy about is that management has been making great strides toward turning the ship around. The firm used the cash from this aforementioned asset sale to pay down debt. But on top of this, operational improvements have boosted expectations for this year in its entirety. Previously, management was expecting a midpoint for revenue for this year of $3.86 billion. But now, it's expected to be around $3.92 billion. On the Topgolf side of things, management expects a $40 million increase compared to prior expectations thanks to improved same venue sales. Those are now expected to decline at only a mid-single-digit rate compared to the 6% to 9% decline previously anticipated. On top of this, core revenue is now expected to be $20 million higher thanks to strong demand for golf equipment.

The firm now also expects EBITDA to be around $500 million at the midpoint. Previously, management expected EBITDA for this year of $460 million. This gap has been bridged by both parts of the company, with each of them seeing a $20 million increase in expectations. In the case of Topgolf, this rise is thanks to higher revenue flow-through. Meanwhile, for the core operations of the business, the $20 million increase can be chalked up to that same factor as well as to continued cost management initiatives that management has been successful in enacting.

For this year in its entirety, I would say that things are going quite well, which is a lot to say considering the impact that tariffs should have on the business. I already mentioned some of that impact. But according to the company, the bottom-line impact from tariffs should be around $40 million for 2025. This is certainly discouraging, to say the least, and in this political environment, I doubt that picture is going to change any time soon. But the good news is that, even if tariffs remain in place and impact the company to this extent moving forward, there is some nice upside here that can be captured.

In the chart above, you can see how shares of Topgolf Callaway Brands are valued using estimates for this year and historical results for last year. Clearly, this is a business that's trading at very low multiples. And often, that means some nice upside potential. But to really understand how much, we should run some additional numbers. In the table below, you can see the total EBITDA expected for the company for this fiscal year. This then strips out Topgolf and also removes $25 million of profitability that management had previously guided for standalone costs for Callaway. That same table goes through and calculates the effective EV to EBITDA multiple of the company in the event that Topgolf is sold for $1 billion, like the rumors have said.

To put in perspective what this means for the business, I would like to point you to the previous chart. In it, you can see how Callaway stacks up against competitor Acushnet Holdings (GOLF) on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. The difference here doesn't seem all that significant. In the chart below, you can see a range of upside and downside based on the multiples that Callaway could trade at. At an EV to EBITDA multiple of only 10, one side would be about 10.2%. That's material, but not world-ending. On the other hand, a multiple of 13 would imply upside of 15.2%, while a multiple of 15 would imply upside of 32.1%.

I would make the case that, after any sort of asset sale, Callaway might well be worth quite a bit more than what Acushnet Holdings is currently trading for. Keep in mind that this competitor has net debt of $812.2 million. That's actually a little lower than the $889.5 million of net debt that Topgolf Callaway Brands has on its books. But when we factor in the estimated $1 billion in Topgolf proceeds, we would turn from having a net debt position to having a net cash position that measures $110.5 million. This additional flexibility makes the company a lot less risky, and it would mean that management has a lot of optionality at its disposal in order to grow. To me, this deserves a premium of sorts.

In the event that Callaway is able to improve bottom-line results following any sort of asset sale, the upside could be massive. In the table above, you can see certain revenue metrics and market value metrics for Callaway on a standalone basis. That same table shows the implied EV to revenue and market cap to revenue multiples of not only Callaway on a standalone basis but also of Acushnet Holdings. Using these metrics, the latter is trading around two times what our prospect is trading for. As the chart below illustrates, matching these multiples would give us upside of between 90.7% and 129.5%. That's drastically higher than the 18.6% increase that we get if we assume that Callaway should trade at the same EV to EBITDA multiple that Acushnet Holdings trades at. And even though the company is going to have to prove itself on the bottom line, the aforementioned flexibility caused by the sea change that it should see in its balance sheet is a fantastic place to start.

Takeaway

The way I see it, investors are finally starting to wake up to the opportunity that Topgolf Callaway Brands offers. Selling off Topgolf is unfortunately regrettable. But I understand why management is doing it. There has become a real disconnect between fundamentals and share price performance, and with same-venue sales declining leading up to this point, it's obvious that the market lost faith in management. Undoing the acquisition, even at a loss, and allocating all future efforts toward the high-quality Callaway brand is the safest way to create shareholder value. And based on my own assessment, there is reason to believe that once management can start doing this, further upside will be on the table. And because of that, I don't mind maintaining it as a 'strong buy' candidate right now.

data.title: What's Driving The Pullback?
data.description:
In the second half of October, the financial chattering class seized onto what seemed like a throwaway comment by OpenAI head Sam Altman during an interview. Responding to a question, Altman acknowledged that the AI space in the stock market was probably in bubble territory, though adding that while the space would probably go through some short-term pain, as generally happens with new technologies, the long-term use case remained extremely robust (it would be hard to imagine Altman, arguably the world’s leading evangelist for the bright shiny future of AI, to say otherwise).

In any event, financial pundits seized onto the “bubble” idea and ran with it. Investors for their part mostly ignored the bubble hype, though, and by the end of the month both the S&P 500 and the S&P Small-Cap Growth index (where a number of the up-and-coming names in the space exist while they try to establish proof of concept) had reached their year-to-date highs.

Shortly thereafter, however, the small cap index started to take a pronounced tumble. Yesterday the S&P 500 followed suit, and if Friday’s market open is any indication of how things end (which may or may not be the case, because even the future six hours ahead is unknowable), the blue chip index will end up down for the week.

Bad Vibes Take Charge

AI-related stocks (in both their large-cap and small-cap manifestations) have certainly taken much of the brunt for this week’s pullback, but the story is broader than that. The economic news – the data points that remain available during this prolonged absence of input from government agencies – has skewed to the downside.

A weekly payroll estimates report from ADP reported an average decrease of 11,250 jobs per week during the four weeks up to October 25, adding support to last week’s job cuts report by Challenger, Gray and Christmas noting October’s large number of losses.

Then the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index came in at 98.2, below expectations and far below the almost giddy optimism small business owners reflected in the period immediately following last year’s presidential election. Small business owners have a great deal to worry about, from labor market concerns to tariffs and the lack of sufficient capital to pony up in today’s increasingly pay-to-play business-government nexus.

There has been a great deal of Fedspeak this week as well, with a number of FOMC voting members opining about the upcoming December meeting, and the market takeaway from much of the Fed commentary has tilted hawkish. The likelihood of a December rate cut, which a couple of weeks ago was seen as a very high probability, is now more or less a coin flip.

Along with the diminishing chances for a rate cut, the divergence of views among committee members, resulting in a string of dissents at recent FOMC meetings, continues to be a subject of concern.

Even with the government reopening this week, the question of when data from the BLS and BEA will become available remains unknown. Economic spokespersons from the White House do not seem at all eager to release a full set of data any time soon, leading to questions about what that data might tell us about the key issues of jobs and inflation.

Seasonality Still a Plus

For all the drama of Thursday’s big price moves on the major markets (with the potential for more on Friday), the pullback thus far remains well short of a bull market correction (ten percent drop from the last peak) or even the five percent fall that we chronicle internally as a notable sentiment shift.

We are still in the thick of what is frequently the most benevolent period of the year for stocks, with the holiday shopping season well underway (shouldn’t we just admit that now every day in November is Black Friday?) and window dressing as investment managers spruce up their portfolios before year-end.

That may still be enough to keep the headwinds of the purported AI bubble, downbeat macro data points, and hawkish Fedspeak at bay. But there is plenty of volatility out there, and we have to be prepared for more twists and turns ahead as we head into 2026. For portfolios with long-term objectives, this is a time to remain disciplined and unswayed by the day-to-day noise.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
